text: Anytime someone gives me the opportunity to be Guy Voller, I'm going to take that opportunity. It's just you can't pass that up.
I'm going to talk about peristomal hernia repair.
In the middle of this, I actually have Guy's slides, and so we'll go into his topic kind of in the middle of my talk here as well.
These are my disclosures, which don't have any relevance to what we're going to talk about.
I don't think I have to tell this audience that peristomal hernias are common. They affect up to fifty percent of stomas.
I think if you get CAT scans or look for people, you will find people with non-symptomatic peristomal hernias at a much higher percentage than that.
We know that these are technically difficult to repair, that they do have a higher surgical site occurrence rate, a higher recurrence rate as well.
And you can add into this the problems of stoma-related complications such as obstruction at the stoma site, injury to the stoma site, necrosis, and kinking from stoma repairs.
We also know that midline hernias are very, very commonly found with these as well.
So when you put this constellation together, this is a problem that we do not have yet a very good solution to.
There's lots of options for repairing these with mesh. We do know that mesh-based repairs are the preferred way to fix these.
Keyhole repair can be done as an intra-abdominal, an onlay, or as a retromuscular repair.

You can sugar baker the mesh, and for a while I think sugar baker was confined to the intraperitoneal space. We're going to show you a little bit at the end how we've moved that into the retromuscular plane.
You can also cruciate the mesh in any number of locations, and I'll touch on that a little bit as part of Guy's talk.
My repair algorithm for peristomals is basically the following.
I reserve laparoscopic sugar baker repairs for those that are relatively small peristomal hernias or that have a smaller or minimal midline component.

Once somebody has a large peristomal hernia or has a moderate or large midline hernia in combination with it, I prefer to do a posterior component separation, typically using a transversus abdominis release.
Your options for doing that include moving the ostomy to a new location or cruciating the mesh or utilizing a stapled ostomy reinforcement procedure.
If the ostomy can't be relocated, you can keyhole the mesh around it, but literature supports not keyholing meshes as often as possible.

And what I'm going to talk about today is sort of our newest modification of this algorithm, which is to intentionally leave the ostomy where it is presently located and retromuscularize it utilizing a PPHR technique.
So how do we do a TAR with an ostomy in sight? Well, the first thing you have to realize is you've seen several videos of TARs here.
When you do a TAR and there's an ostomy that you're taking down, there will be a defect in the posterior layer that's just part and parcel of the operation, and you simply sew that up.
The surgeon begins releasing the transversus abdominis muscle in the upper abdomen, a step that can be performed robotically as demonstrated in previous videos.
Those are the best videos you're going to see because you get a very good view. This is hard to show in an open technique.

We typically, in the older version of this operation, would move the ostomy to a new location. So we close the old stoma site in the posterior sheath, we move the ostomy to a new site, and you create a small cruciate in the posterior layer of the reconstruction. to deliver the stoma through.
The surgeon places mesh over the repair site and creates a cruciate incision in the mesh to deliver the stoma through. The video provides an on-floss view of the completed repair.

The video shows the old ostomy site on the left-hand side, which is widely reinforced with mesh. The midline is also reinforced with mesh, and the mesh has been cut to allow the ostomy to pass through.

This is what it looks like in a cross-section, and what I like about this slide is that it does show you that if you're repairing a midline defect, your mesh has been cut where the new ostomy has been brought through, oftentimes within three or four centimeters of the midline. That could theoretically compromise the midline repair, and while the new ostomy site is reinforced with mesh, again, the mesh has been cut at that location to allow the bowel to be delivered through, so there's a theoretical risk of getting a hernia back at that site.

Mike Rosen and Yuri Novitskiy published their initial experience with this technique. Seventy percent of their patients in this series had a midline incisional hernia as well, which gave them a very large hernia area of almost two hundred and thirty centimeters in these patients. At thirteen months, they had eleven percent recurrence rate, and most of those recurrences were located at the new ostomy site.
Whether you want to call that a new hernia or a recurrent hernia, it's neither here nor there. The patient had a peristomal hernia, and they have a new peristomal hernia that requires a repair.

They had the anticipated amount of postoperative problems, including deep and superficial surgical site infections. They had one mucocutaneous separation. Again, these are problems inherent to managing people who have an ostomy. And I will point out that they had one patient with an ischemic stoma in that series.

But I think ultimately that repair gives you a very acceptable risk of recurrence. It does relocate the ostomy. The new site is reinforced. You get a Y sublay overlap. You can repair midline defects, and I think there's pretty good support that it's safe to use polypropylene mesh in those circumstances.
However, the ostomy does need to be moved. So now you've got an old wound site and a new wound site. You've transected the bowel. You've mobilized it to a new location.

It's probably a little bit more contaminated than if you simply covered the ostomy and left it in place. and the mesh is cut, and as I said earlier, most of those hernias happen where the ostomy site was located.

In particular, in this case, the other problem is that mesh configuration is tricky. You're trying to make a hole in the posterior sheath in the mesh and in the new ostomy site in the native abdominal wall, and lining those three spots up with one another can be very tricky.

To help make that easier, people have looked at this idea of stapled reinforcement technique or a smart technique, and these were guy slides. This was first described in two thousand eleven as a way to prophylax ostomy sites against a hernia recurrence. It's a very easy way to place the mesh.

It prevents the abdominal wall and the mesh in the new ostomy location from sliding or kinking on one another. and really prevents a lot of mesh migration toward the location where the bowel is located while the repair is healing. You really don't have to stretch the muscle because you're using a stapler to do it.

And while they initially described this with a very specially designed stapler, you can simply do this with any EEA stapler. These are the pictures from their original description of the technique, where they've made a small hole in the subcutaneous layer.
The surgeon uses the anvil of an EEA stapler to deliver the mesh into the bowel through the opening created in the abdominal wall, aligning the mesh with the ostomy site.

There have been subsequent reports on this. In hernia in two thousand fifteen, Williams reported twenty-two patients. There were a combination of old and new ostomy sites. With a median follow-up of twenty-one months, they reported nineteen percent paralysomal hernia recurrence with the SMART technique and seventy-three percent if you just relocated.

So good evidence for using this to prophylax. In a more recent study in two thousand seventeen, fourteen patients had the same technique prophylaxis for colon sites that were created. They had two recurrences with a median follow up of two years.

I personally wrote a little rebuttal to this article just sort of criticizing the technique a little bit. I kind of disagree with using a stapler to resect portions of the native abdominal wall. And while the idea that stapling the mesh to the abdominal wall helps it and theoretically prevents that size from being open, I can assure you that that is not the case.

This is a patient from my institution. I have a surgeon who'd been doing this technique long before the original descriptions of it. He simply never published it. And so this is onlay polypropylene mesh, and this was a twenty-one millimeter EEA stapler site, which now measures almost four millimeters in size, four centimeters in size. and now has a peristomal hernia associated with it. So the staples, they're not interconnected.
They don't prevent that ostomy site from expanding over the course of time. And so I don't know in the long term whether this here is not a good idea.

Yuri Novitskiy has published utilizing a TAR technique with what he described as a STORM or a stapled transabdominal ostomy reinforcement. This is essentially the way, instead of cruciating the mesh where the new ostomy is going to be located with that TAR procedure that I showed you earlier, of rather utilizing the stapler to create that trophene.
So these are Yuri's slides. I'm giving guys talk with Yuri's slides. I kind of like this. The tar is performed in the upper abdomen, similar to the technique you've seen earlier.

The surgeon brings the bowel through the posterior sheath and positions it at the planned site for the new ostomy. The layers are aligned meticulously, highlighting the intricate nature of this repair.

The surgeon ensures that all three layers of the abdominal wall are aligned before making incisions. An anvil is placed through the posterior sheath and mesh, connecting through the abdominal wall to secure all layers together.
The surgeon fires the stapler, allowing the bowel to be delivered through the newly created ostomy site.

They used synthetic mesh the majority of the time. They had the anticipated amount of surgical site events for a peristomal repair, and they did have two recurrences within a year of follow-up. So on par with what they had already previously published, You can also do the same technique in an onlay.
The video shows an onlay mesh stapled and fibrin sealed to the abdominal wall. The trophene with stapled reinforcement is visible, and the bowel is delivered through the onlay trophene.

Whether or not this prevents recurrences, I don't know. But if you're doing a TAR and are moving the ostomy to a new location, I think it is a very nice way to make sure that those three layers are lined up, you know, in a very precise fashion, that they don't have the opportunity to migrate or shift in the very early postoperative period. So I think for that purpose, it clearly makes sense.

What we have described, and I think what other people are doing now, is essentially a tar, like I showed you, but rather than moving the stoma, the stoma is retromuscularized, utilizing what is essentially a sugar baker configuration. So this is a tar and a sugar baker put together into one operation. The mesh does not need to be cut. It's a sugar baker configuration mesh.

The stoma does not need to be relocated. So it addresses some of the problems that we described earlier with moving the ostomy and doing a tar. I will say that having done a fair number of these now, it's very difficult to configure the mesh around the bowel. and there are some pretty severe consequences to doing that poorly, as I'll show you at the end here.
The surgeon demonstrates a retromuscular dissection on a cadaver model, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon performs a retrorectus release while leaving the ostomy in place, followed by a transversus abdominis release around the ostomy.

The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) around the stoma, leaving the ostomy in place. This procedure requires careful manipulation, particularly in obese patients, to ensure proper alignment and integration of the stoma within the retromuscular space.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the linea semilunaris and the ostomy while working on a far lateralized stoma. A deliberate defect is created in the posterior sheath, highlighting a critical aspect of the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.
The surgeon intentionally creates a hole in the posterior sheath to reposition the bowel from a medial location to a lateral position. The defect is then sutured from lateral to medial.
The bowel is repositioned from its entry site in the posterior rectus sheath into the retromuscular plane, moving laterally into the preperitoneal or pre-transversalis space.

The surgeon positions the bowel in a Sugarbaker configuration, with the peritoneum visible below. The posterior rectus sheath is closed in the midline, and if a large midline defect is present, a transversus abdominis release (TAR) can be performed on the opposite side.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of mesh within the large sublay space created during the procedure, highlighting different types of mesh options available for use.

Just like when we first started doing these, we were placing sutures around the side, just like you would do in any other sugar baker repair. I have stopped doing that. There's really, I think, a relatively low risk of bowel creeping around the sides, and just like
Igor, who said that he stopped with trans-fascial fixation. I simply placed the mesh very far lateral and either used fiber and sealant or really no fixation to allow it to stay in situ.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of intermediate-weight polypropylene mesh in a wide overlap configuration over the midline defect, ensuring no mesh is cut within multiple centimeters of the midline. A cross-sectional view reveals the spatial arrangement of the mesh and bowel.

The surgeon enters the retromuscular plane on the patient's far right side, visible on the screen's left, and progresses in a classic Sugarbaker configuration towards the midline. An on FOSS view of this configuration is shown.

This is a video that I stole from Jeff Blacknick who showed this two weeks ago at AHS. This is him doing a robot version of this exact same operation. In the same way Igor can do a TAR laparoscopically and Alfie can do one robotically, you can do this operation utilizing robotic technique. He's already taken down the posterior sheath and done the TAR around the ostomy.

The surgeon lateralizes the mesh and closes the posterior layer, measuring the mesh size to ensure adequate overlap over the peristomal defect. Trans-fascial fixation sutures are placed on the right and left sides for stabilization.

The mesh is positioned in situ in a Sugarbaker configuration. The surgeon prepares to perform a double docking maneuver, placing the robotic system on the ipsilateral side to the ostomy. The mesh is unrolled in the same manner as previously demonstrated, addressing the patient's peristomal and multiple midline hernias.

Our initial experience, which we presented at AHS a year ago, was ten patients with a pretty good mean defect size. We had colostomies, ileostomies, and several urostomies as well. And with our thirty-day follow-up at that point, we had really no stoma complications, mesh erosions, and hernia recurrences. And we've got multiple patients out beyond a year.

We're trying to get a much larger series of a hundred patients with a year of follow-up. Having said that, I'm going to, here's what this looks like in the short term. So, A is the pre-procedure and B is the post-procedure. At thirty days, you can see the bowel entering the retromuscular plane in a very shallow angle. This is the same patient at one year follow-up, the same thing. You can see the colon, in this case, entering the retromuscular plane at a very shallow angle and no hernia recurrence.

The video shows a patient with a large midline defect and a small peristomal defect. The midline defect is visible before and after closure, with the stoma site lateralized and reinforced. The patient is shown before and after the repair, now a year post-operation.

I'm going to leave this slide up here and let this sink in for a minute. This is one of Mike Rosen's patients that he posted on the International Hernia Collaboration who had this operation who presented six months, within the first six months post-op, with a necrotic and ischemic ostomy as a result of MESH, erosion into the mesentery.

You know, there's a lot of things about this patient that make it a challenging patient. It's a Crohn's patient with a multiply recurrent hernia. You know, ultimately, mesh configuration around any ostomy can result in problems, and this operation is ultimately no different than that.

So I think before everybody goes home and starts doing this, you know, the jury is still out on this as well, just like a guy concluded I'm going to conclude the same thing. This may be a good idea, this may be a really terrible idea, but I'm going to leave this slide up so that we all at least recognize that it may be a problem.
Thank you very much.
